title: The first memory
description: The project began not with a question but with a scent — revisiting childhood memories of nano's garden, where motia grew in earthen pots by the courtyard wall. This early reflection became the seed of the entire inquiry.

title: Visiting nano
description: A visit to my grandmother's home in Pakistan. I watched her tend to the jasmine plants as she always had — watering them at dusk, picking the buds just before they opened. I recorded our conversations about the flower's meaning in her daily life.

title: Gathering jasmine
description: I began collecting motia flowers from local South Asian gardens and florists. Each batch was pressed, dried, and catalogued. The act of gathering became a ritual in itself — a way of learning the flower through touch.

title: Reading and research
description: Immersion in cultural studies scholarship — reading Sara Ahmed on affect, Trinh T. Minh-ha on sensory ethnography, and ethnobotanical texts on jasmine's role across South Asian ritual life. The theoretical framework began to take shape.

title: Oral histories with my mother
description: A series of recorded conversations with my mother about her relationship with motia — how she wore it in her hair as a young woman, how she associated it with her own mother, how the scent travelled with her when she left home.

title: Making garlands
description: Learning the technique of threading motia into garlands — the traditional gajra. My hands learned what my grandmother's hands already knew. The repetition became meditative, a form of embodied research.

title: Developing the textile concept
description: The collection concept emerged: garments that carry the motia's qualities — its off-white softness, its brief lifespan, the gesture of tending and wearing. Initial sketches explored draping, layering, and hand-stitching as metaphors for care.

title: Pattern making and toiles
description: Translating the concept into pattern. Working with natural fabrics — cotton, silk organza, handwoven khadi — to find the right weight and fall. Each toile was a conversation between the idea and the material.

title: The collection takes form
description: Six pieces completed: each named after a quality of the motia flower. The garments sit between traditional South Asian silhouettes and contemporary minimalism — quiet, warm, and deliberately impermanent in feeling.

title: Photoshoot preparation
description: Scouting locations that echo the domestic warmth of nano's home — a sunlit courtyard, a linen-draped room, a garden at golden hour. Collaborating with a photographer who understood the project's quiet visual language.

title: The photoshoot
description: Two days of shooting. Fresh motia flowers were present on set — their scent filled the space. The model wore the garments as they were meant to be worn: gently, with the slow care of someone dressing for no one but herself.

title: Writing the abstract
description: Distilling the research into its final written form. The abstract became a space where the personal and the scholarly met — a reflection on what it means to study something you have always carried.

title: Completing the artwork
description: The final body of artwork — pressed flower compositions, textile samples, and photographic prints — was assembled. Together with the written thesis, it forms a complete practice-led inquiry into memory, scent, and inheritance.

title: Thesis submission
description: The written thesis and practice portfolio were submitted. The project ends where it began — with a flower, a scent, and the quiet knowledge that some things are inherited without ever being spoken.
